text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY - FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Department of Hematology/Oncology

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: [Attending Oncologist]
REFERRING PROVIDER: [Primary Care Physician]
PATIENT: Raymond Kowalski
DOB: [on file]
MRN: [on file]
DIAGNOSIS: Metastatic prostate adenocarcinoma, Gleason 9 (5+4), AJCC Stage IVB (M1b), BRCA2 germline pathogenic variant
VISIT TYPE: Follow-up, Established Patient

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Kowalski is a 69-year-old Polish-American male, retired steelworker, presenting for oncology follow-up in the context of a rising prostate-specific antigen (PSA) over the past three months. He was recently noted on labs ordered by his primary care physician to have a PSA of 8.7 ng/mL, representing a significant rise from his nadir. He also reports new onset low back pain over the past six weeks.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski was diagnosed approximately two years ago with de novo metastatic castration-sensitive prostate cancer (mCSPC), stage IVB, with a presenting PSA of 187 ng/mL, Gleason score 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5. Staging at diagnosis revealed bilateral pelvic lymphadenopathy (right obturator node 1.8 cm, left external iliac node 1.4 cm) and 8 discrete bone metastases on technetium bone scan, including L2 vertebral body, T9 vertebral body, left iliac wing, right acetabulum, right femoral neck, and bilateral rib lesions. He also harbors a germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (p.E1308*, confirmed on blood germline testing), and his tumor molecular profile was notable for PTEN homozygous deletion, RB1 loss, TP53 p.R248Q, and CDK12 frameshift mutation. No AR amplification or AR LBD mutations were identified at the time of diagnosis.

He was initiated on combined androgen deprivation therapy with leup acetate (Lupron Depot 22.5 mg IM q3 months) plus Zytiga acetate (Zytiga) 1000 mg daily with prednisone 5 mg BID for mCSPC, in accordance with LATITUDE/STAMPEDE trial data. Taxotere was discussed at that time but deferred given patient preference and his underlying stage 3 chronic kidney disease (baseline creatinine ~1.4 mg/dL, eGFR ~52 mL/min).

His initial response was excellent: PSA declined from 187 ng/mL to a nadir of 1.1 ng/mL at approximately 14 months into treatment, and a restaging bone scan at 6 months showed marked improvement in osseous disease with regression of lymphadenopathy. Testosterone was confirmed castrate (18-22 ng/dL) throughout.

However, over the past three to four months, PSA has been rising on serial measurements:
- PSA approximately 5 months ago: 1.9 ng/mL (first rise above nadir)
- PSA approximately 2 months ago: 4.8 ng/mL
- PSA at today's visit (confirmed on repeat draw from this institution): 8.7 ng/mL
- Testosterone today: 22 ng/dL (confirmed castrate)

This pattern of two or more consecutive rises with castrate testosterone, with the PSA now exceeding 2 ng/mL above nadir on at least two measurements, is consistent with castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC) by PCWG3 criteria. He has been on abi for approximately 24 months total.

In addition to the biochemical progression, Mr. Kowalski reports the development of new low back pain over the past six weeks, localizing primarily to the lumbar region. He describes it as a constant, dull aching sensation, rated 3/10 in intensity at rest and up to 5/10 with prolonged standing or walking. He denies radiation of pain into either lower extremity. He denies any new numbness, tingling, weakness, or changes in bowel or bladder function that would suggest cord compression or cauda equina syndrome. He has been managing with acetaminophen 1g TID with partial relief.

He denies new bone pain in any other location. He denies fevers, chills, night sweats, unintentional weight loss, hemoptysis, dyspnea, palpitations, or chest pain. Appetite is mildly decreased but he is maintaining his weight. He has not had any falls. He remains ambulatory without assistive devices.

His hot flashes, which were previously grade 2, have stabilized on ADT. He has tolerated the prednisone component of his Zytiga regimen without significant corticosteroid-related complications. Blood pressure has been reasonably well controlled on his adjusted antihypertensive regimen. His type 2 diabetes remains managed on metformin.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Mild fatigue, grade 1-2. Appetite slightly reduced. No unintentional weight loss reported at this visit. No fevers, chills, or night sweats.
MSK/Neurologic: New lumbar back pain as described above (6 weeks, grade 2). Denies lower extremity weakness, saddle anesthesia, bowel or bladder incontinence, or bilateral leg pain radiating below the knee. No new focal bone pain in hips, ribs, or shoulder girdle. No headaches.
Respiratory: No cough, dyspnea, or hemoptysis.
Gastrointestinal: Mild decreased appetite. No nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or constipation. No melena or hematochezia. Denies jaundice or abdominal pain.
Genitourinary: Mild obstructive urinary symptoms (weak stream, mild hesitancy) at baseline; no acute worsening. No hematuria.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity swelling. Mild bilateral lower extremity edema at baseline (attributed to amlodipine and prior abi use).
Endocrine/Metabolic: Hot flashes persist, manageable. No symptomatic hypoglycemia. Blood sugars per patient report "doing okay."
Hematologic: No unusual bruising or bleeding.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Alert, cooperative, well-appearing gentleman in no acute distress. Appears stated age. Conversational.
Vitals: Blood pressure 138/84 mmHg (right arm, seated), heart rate 72 bpm, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, SpO2 97% on room air, weight 182 lbs (stable from prior visit), temperature 36.8 degrees C.
HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae non-icteric. Oropharynx clear.
Neck: Supple. No palpable cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary lymphadenopathy.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No JVD.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, crackles, or rhonchi.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No organomegaly appreciated. No palpable masses. Bowel sounds active.
Musculoskeletal: Lumbar spine with mild midline tenderness to palpation at L2-L3 level. No paraspinal spasm. No focal tenderness over the sacrum or iliac crests. Range of motion of lumbar spine mildly limited by pain. Bilateral hip range of motion preserved without focal bony tenderness. No gross extremity joint swelling.
Neurologic: Alert and oriented x3. Motor strength 5/5 in bilateral upper and lower extremities. Sensation grossly intact to light touch in bilateral lower extremities. Reflexes 2+ patellae bilaterally, 2+ Achilles bilaterally. Gait: mildly antalgic secondary to back pain, but steady without assistive device.
Skin: No rash. No ecchymoses. Mild bilateral pretibial edema, 1+, pitting.
Lymph nodes: No inguinal lymphadenopathy palpable.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

PSA: 8.7 ng/mL (previous nadir 1.1 ng/mL; confirmed rising x2 prior values)
Testosterone: 22 ng/dL (castrate range confirmed)
CBC:
  - Hemoglobin: 10.9 g/dL (mild, normocytic anemia; consistent with prior trend on ADT)
  - Hematocrit: 33.1%
  - MCV: 88 fL
  - Platelets: 218,000/uL
  - WBC: 7.4 K/uL; ANC 4.8 K/uL
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  - Sodium: 138 mEq/L
  - Potassium: 4.2 mEq/L
  - Creatinine: 1.6 mg/dL (mild rise from baseline 1.4; eGFR estimated ~48 mL/min per CKD-EPI)
  - BUN: 26 mg/dL
  - Glucose: 118 mg/dL (mildly elevated, consistent with known DM2 and prednisone use)
  - Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL
  - ALT: 28 U/L
  - AST: 34 U/L
  - Alkaline phosphatase (ALP): 221 U/L (elevated; increased from 98 U/L at last visit, suggesting new or worsening osseous disease)
  - Albumin: 3.5 g/dL
LDH: 248 U/L (mildly elevated)
HbA1c: 7.4% (adequate diabetes control)
PT/INR: 1.1 (normal)

---

IMAGING RESULTS

No new imaging available at today's visit. The most recent imaging was approximately 18 months prior, at which time a restaging bone scan and CT abdomen/pelvis demonstrated significant response to ADT + abi, with regression of most osseous lesions and normalization of pelvic lymphadenopathy. Given the new biochemical and clinical evidence of progression, restaging imaging is being ordered today (see plan).

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. Kowalski is a 69-year-old male with stage IVB (M1b) metastatic prostate adenocarcinoma, Gleason 9 (5+4), with a germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (p.E1308*) and high-risk molecular features (PTEN loss, RB1 loss, TP53 mutation), now presenting with biochemical and likely clinical progression consistent with castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC). Key findings today include:

1. PSA rising to 8.7 ng/mL from nadir of 1.1 ng/mL, with testosterone confirmed castrate at 22 ng/dL - this meets PCWG3 definition of PSA progression in the castrate setting (two rising values, each at least 1 week apart, with the last value ≥2 ng/mL above nadir). Duration on Zytiga: approximately 24 months - a reasonable run for this agent in the mCSPC setting.

2. New lumbar back pain over the past 6 weeks: given known bone metastases including prior involvement of L2 and T9 vertebral bodies, this is highly concerning for progressive osseous disease. The clinical examination is reassuring in that there are no signs of impending cord compression (no radiculopathy, motor or sensory deficits, or sphincter dysfunction), but close monitoring is warranted.

3. Rising ALP (221 U/L from prior 98 U/L) is further corroborating evidence of disease progression, likely bone origin.

Problem-Oriented Plan:

1. CASTRATION-RESISTANT PROSTATE CANCER - CONFIRMED PROGRESSION
   - Zytiga acetate and prednisone are being discontinued today given documented PSA progression in the castrate setting - there is no benefit to continuing this agent in the face of confirmed CRPC progression.
   - leup acetate (Lupron Depot) will be CONTINUED to maintain castrate testosterone levels. Continuation of LHRH agonist therapy is standard of care throughout subsequent lines of treatment in mCRPC.
   - RESTAGING IMAGING ORDERED:
     a. Technetium-99m bone scan (whole body) to assess extent of osseous disease and identify any new lesions
     b. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast to evaluate lymphadenopathy, visceral organs, and interval change from prior imaging
     Results are anticipated within the next 7-10 days. A follow-up appointment has been scheduled for approximately 2 weeks to review imaging and finalize next-line systemic therapy.
   - NEXT-LINE THERAPY CONSIDERATIONS: Given confirmed mCRPC after abi failure, the following options were discussed with Mr. Kowalski at today's visit:
     a. enza (Xtandi) 160 mg orally daily: A second-generation androgen receptor signaling inhibitor with Level 1 evidence in mCRPC (PREVAIL trial and others). Despite the prior Zytiga use, Xtandi acts through a distinct mechanism (direct AR antagonism, nuclear translocation inhibition) and retains activity in a subset of patients post-Zytiga. This is a favored option given the patient's performance status and oral tolerability.
     b. Taxotere (Taxotere)-based chemotherapy: Remains a standard option in mCRPC with Level 1 evidence (TAX327). However, given his CKD (eGFR ~48), borderline hemoglobin (10.9 g/dL), and patient preference to avoid IV chemotherapy for now, this is deferred.
     c. ola (Lynparza): Given the confirmed germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant, Mr. Kowalski is potentially eligible for PARP inhibitor therapy. The PROfound trial demonstrated rPFS benefit for Lynparza in mCRPC patients with HRR gene alterations, particularly BRCA1/2. However, the current FDA label for ola in mCRPC specifies it should follow prior treatment with a novel hormonal agent (NHA), and many would proceed with a second NHA first. We will revisit ola eligibility in the context of imaging findings and the patient's evolving molecular profile.
     d. Cabazitaxel (Jevtana): Reserved for post-Taxotere mCRPC; not applicable at this stage.
     e. Lutetium-177 PSMA-617 (Pluvicto): PSMA PET imaging has not been performed; this would require gallium-68 PSMA PET qualification. May be considered in subsequent lines.
   - PENDING IMAGING: Will not initiate next-line systemic therapy until restaging imaging reviewed. If imaging confirms bone-only or bone/nodal-limited disease without visceral involvement, enza will be the planned next-line therapy.
   - GERMLINE BRCA2: Patient was previously counseled and connected with genetic counseling. The BRCA2 germline pathogenic variant has implications not only for his own management (PARP inhibitor eligibility) but also for first-degree relatives (familial implications). Ensured patient recalls this context and offered re-referral to genetic counseling as needed.

2. BONE PAIN - LUMBAR REGION
   - New onset low back pain in the setting of known L2 vertebral body metastasis is concerning for disease progression at this site. We will await bone scan results.
   - URGENT SPINE MRI: Given the new lumbar pain and known vertebral body involvement, I have ordered an MRI of the thoracic and lumbar spine with and without gadolinium to evaluate for epidural extension, cord compromise, or pathologic fracture risk. This is being ordered today as a priority study with results expected within 48-72 hours. The patient and his wife have been instructed that if he develops any new neurological symptoms (leg weakness, numbness in groin/perineum/inner thighs, loss of bowel or bladder control), he should go directly to the emergency department.
   - Continue acetaminophen 1g TID as needed for pain.
   - Low-threshold to add a short-acting opioid if pain escalates prior to next visit. Discussed with patient - he is not ready for opioid initiation at this time.
   - Zoledronic acid (Zometa) for bone protection: Patient has been on ADT for 2 years and has known bone metastases. Initiation of bone-modifying agent therapy (zoledronic acid 4 mg IV q12 weeks, dose-adjusted for CKD per eGFR) to be discussed at next visit pending imaging results and renal function stability. Dental evaluation to be recommended prior to initiation (osteonecrosis of the jaw prophylaxis).

3. ANEMIA
   - Hemoglobin 10.9 g/dL (mild normocytic anemia), consistent with prior trend attributed to ADT-related suppression and chronic disease. No transfusion required at this time. Iron studies were obtained previously and were unremarkable for iron deficiency. Will recheck CBC at next visit.

4. CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE (STAGE 3A)
   - Creatinine mildly worsened to 1.6 mg/dL (eGFR ~48). Will continue to monitor. Dose adjustments for any renally cleared agents will be made accordingly. Metformin is continued given eGFR >45 but patient counseled to hold if he undergoes contrast imaging (iodinated contrast for CT scan - renal precautions discussed).
   - Adequate IV hydration to be used for contrast-enhanced CT. Pre-hydration protocol ordered.

5. TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS
   - HbA1c 7.4%, reasonably controlled. Stopping prednisone (which was prescribed with abi) may improve glycemic control going forward. Will monitor. Continue metformin 1000 mg BID.

6. HYPERTENSION
   - Blood pressure 138/84 mmHg today, adequate. Currently on lisinopril 20 mg daily and amlodipine 10 mg daily. Continue current regimen.

PATIENT EDUCATION AND COUNSELING:
Extensive time was spent with Mr. Kowalski and his wife explaining the concept of castration resistance - that while his cancer initially responded beautifully to leup plus Zytiga, the cancer has now found ways to continue growing despite low testosterone levels. This is a known and anticipated trajectory for many men with high-grade, widely metastatic prostate cancer, and does not mean that treatment has failed irreversibly. We discussed that there are multiple effective subsequent lines of therapy available and that we expect to be able to maintain meaningful disease control.

The importance of the BRCA2 mutation was revisited in the context of future PARP inhibitor eligibility (ola), which may be offered in a subsequent line. The patient expressed understanding and is motivated to continue pursuing active treatment.

He and his wife were given my direct nursing line contact information and instructed to call immediately for any new neurological symptoms given the spinal lesions. Safety netting provided regarding cord compression precautions.

FOLLOW-UP: Return appointment in 2 weeks for imaging review and treatment decision. Spine MRI results to be communicated to the patient by phone within 72 hours of completion.

Time spent with patient: approximately 55 minutes (>50% counseling and coordination of care).

[Electronically signed by Attending Oncologist]
